content.text: Tech Radar Rings for Claude Ecosystem Tools

A Technology Radar organizes tools, platforms, and techniques into four rings — Adopt, Trial, Assess, and Hold — to give engineering teams unambiguous guidance about what to use and what to avoid. Applying the radar model to the Claude ecosystem lets enterprise architecture teams communicate evaluated positions on Anthropic's SDKs, cloud integrations, agentic frameworks, and deployment patterns.

The radar does not reflect the absolute quality of a technology — it reflects your organization's evaluated confidence in it given your specific risk tolerance, regulatory environment, and operational maturity.
The Four Rings
Adopt

Tools and techniques in Adopt are production-proven within your organization (or by peers in similar regulatory contexts). They have stable APIs, maintained dependencies, known failure modes, and documented operational runbooks. Engineering teams should use these as the default choice without further evaluation.
Anthropic Python SDK (
anthropic
Versioned, stable, type-annotated, actively maintained. Use for all server-side Claude integrations.
Anthropic TypeScript/Node SDK (
@anthropic-ai/sdk
Same stability guarantees as the Python SDK. Default for Node.js backends.
Claude Messages API:
The core inference endpoint. Stable REST contract with documented versioning policy. Use directly for maximum control.
Tool use / function calling:
Structured tool definitions with JSON Schema constraints. Production-ready; the recommended pattern for agentic tasks.
Streaming responses:
Server-sent events streaming is stable and supported across all official SDKs. Required for interactive UX.
Trial

Trial technologies are ready for limited production use in non-critical workloads or behind feature flags. They have been validated in proofs of concept, but lack the operational history to recommend universally. Each team adopting a Trial technology should run a time-boxed pilot with defined success criteria and report findings back to the architecture board.
Claude Code (CLI):
AI coding assistant with agentic capabilities. Production-ready for developer tooling workflows; evaluate carefully before use in automated pipelines.
AWS Bedrock — Claude models:
VPC-native deployment removes egress concerns and enables PrivateLink. Evaluate for data-residency-sensitive workloads. Some model versions lag behind direct API availability.
Prompt caching:
Reduces latency and cost for repeated large context. API is stable; cache invalidation semantics require validation against your specific prompt patterns.
Batch API:
Async bulk processing at lower cost. Evaluate for high-volume offline workloads. SLA and failure-mode documentation still maturing.
Claude on Google Cloud Vertex AI:
Enterprise SLA and GCP-native IAM. Evaluate for GCP-primary organizations. Feature parity with direct API is approaching but not yet complete.
Assess

Assess technologies are promising but not yet ready for production adoption. They warrant focused exploration — prototyping, reading documentation, attending vendor briefings — to build organizational understanding. Do not adopt Assess technologies in production without explicit architecture board approval.
Extended thinking (
thinking
blocks):
Claude's extended reasoning mode for complex multi-step problems. Still maturing in terms of latency predictability and cost modeling; evaluate for specific high-stakes use cases.
Agentic orchestration frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex):
Useful abstractions for RAG pipelines and multi-step workflows, but introduce significant hidden complexity and version churn. Assess whether the abstraction layer adds value over direct SDK usage.
AutoRAG / managed retrieval services:
Managed knowledge base with automated embedding and retrieval. Evaluate for teams without RAG infrastructure expertise; assess data-residency implications.
Claude in multi-agent pipelines:
Using Claude as an orchestrator calling other Claude (or non-Claude) model instances. Powerful but requires careful design of trust boundaries and termination conditions.
MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers:
Emerging standard for giving Claude access to tools and data. Protocol is stabilizing; ecosystem is immature. Assess for greenfield agentic projects.
Hold

Hold does not mean 'never use' — it means 'pause new adoption and evaluate the position before committing further.' Technologies in Hold have identified problems that outweigh their current benefits for enterprise deployment.
Unconstrained agentic loops:

Autonomous agents that can call tools without human confirmation gates and without maximum-iteration limits. Hold until you have implemented proper guardrails (HITL gates, loop termination, sandboxing).
Self-hosted open-source LLMs as Claude replacements:
Attractive for data-residency, but operational burden (GPU infra, model updates, safety alignment) is substantial. Hold pending clearer open-source safety story for enterprise use.
System prompt sharing across tenants:
Using a single shared Claude context for multiple distinct tenants. Fundamental privacy and isolation risk. Hold until tenant isolation is architecturally enforced at the prompt layer.
Unvalidated third-party Claude wrappers:
Thin wrappers over the Claude API from vendors with unclear maintenance commitments. Hold; prefer the official SDK with a thin internal abstraction layer you control.
Quadrant Structure
The Claude ecosystem radar uses two quadrants to group related technologies:
Quadrant
What It Covers
Examples
Tooling & SDKs
Official libraries, CLIs, and developer tools that directly interact with Claude
Python SDK, TypeScript SDK, Claude Code, Workbench
Integrations & Patterns
Cloud platform integrations, architectural patterns, and third-party orchestration frameworks
AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, LangChain, MCP, prompt caching patterns

Keep quadrant boundaries stable across radar editions — teams navigate the radar by quadrant, and reorganizing quadrants creates confusion. Add new quadrants only when a genuinely distinct technology domain emerges (e.g., a 'Evaluation & Safety' quadrant once that tooling matures).
Running the Radar Process
Cadence and Governance
A radar that is never updated loses credibility. Establish a minimum cadence and clear ownership:
Activity
Frequency
Owner
Full radar review and publication
Every 6 months (or after major Anthropic release)
Architecture board
Emergency Hold placement
Within 48h of a security advisory or critical incident
Security architect + Engineering lead
New blip proposal
Any engineer submits via RFC process
Proposing team + Architecture board
Blip promotion (Assess → Trial)
After PoC with documented findings
Team lead + Architecture review
Blip promotion (Trial → Adopt)
After 2+ production deployments with positive ops data
Architecture board vote

attrs.alt: Blip Movement Signals: When to Change Ring Assignments

content.text: Writing a Blip Entry
Each radar entry (blip) should have a structured description that a team can act on in five minutes. Use this template:

## [Tool Name] — [Ring]
**Quadrant:** Tooling & SDKs | Integrations & Patterns
**Movement:** NEW | moved from [ring] | no change

### What it is
One sentence on what the technology does.

### Why this ring
Two to four sentences justifying the ring placement. Reference concrete
evidence: a post-mortem, a benchmark, a successful pilot, a security advisory.

### How to use it (for Adopt/Trial)
Specific guidance: which version, which configuration, which use cases are in
scope and which are out of scope.

### Watch for (for Assess/Hold)
What you are monitoring that would trigger a ring change. Include a timeframe
if possible.

### See also
Links to internal RFCs, PoC repos, or external documentation.
Example Blip Entries
Concrete examples of how blips are written for the Claude ecosystem:

## Anthropic Python SDK (anthropic) — ADOPT
Quadrant: Tooling & SDKs
Movement: no change

### What it is
The official Python client library for the Anthropic Messages API,
supporting synchronous and async usage, streaming, and tool use.

### Why this ring
Used in production across all three platform teams since Q1 2024. API has
been stable through four minor SDK versions with no breaking changes. Type
annotations and automatic retries reduce boilerplate. Engineering survey
shows 94% satisfaction. No CVEs in the dependency tree in the past 12 months.

### How to use it
Pin to ^0.x.x (current major); use AsyncAnthropic for all async contexts.
Prefer sdk-native retry logic (max_retries=3) over manual retry loops.
Do not wrap in an additional abstraction layer — use the SDK directly.

### See also
Internal RFC-2041: SDK standardization | docs.anthropic.com/sdk-reference

## Unconstrained Agentic Loops — HOLD
Quadrant: Integrations & Patterns
Movement: no change (remains HOLD)

### What it is
Agentic Claude deployments where the model can autonomously invoke tools
in a loop without human confirmation gates or maximum-iteration limits.

### Why this ring
Post-mortem from Q3 2024 incident: an unconstrained agent loop invoked
the send_email tool 847 times in 4 minutes before detection, resulting in
a customer notification incident and regulatory disclosure obligation.
Security threat modeling (TC-TA-01) confirms HITL gates are mandatory for
all write-effect tools. No mitigation exists that makes unconstrained loops
safe in our environment without human gates.

### Watch for
We will re-evaluate when Anthropic ships model-level tool invocation limits
and when our HITL gate framework is mature enough to be toggled per-tool.
Target review: Q2 2026.

### See also
Post-mortem PM-2024-Q3-001 | TC-TA-01 threat case | HITL gate RFC-2089
Communicating the Radar
A radar stored in a wiki that nobody reads is useless. Embed it into the engineering workflow:
Radar site:
Publish the radar as an interactive HTML/SVG artefact (Thoughtworks Radar or a custom build) accessible via a memorable internal URL (e.g.,
radar.internal.example.com
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs):
Link each ADR that involves a Claude ecosystem technology to its radar blip. The ADR explains the decision; the radar explains the current evaluated position.
RFC template:
Include a required section: 'Technologies used — radar status' in every RFC proposing new Claude integrations. Proposals using Hold technologies require explicit architecture board exception.
Onboarding:
Reference the radar in new engineer onboarding as the source of truth for which Claude tools to use, so new team members don't independently evaluate already-evaluated tools.
Radar changelog:
Publish a changelog alongside each radar edition summarising which blips moved and why. This is the most-read section; write it carefully.
Radar Anti-Patterns
Anti-Pattern
Why It Harms the Radar's Value
Fix
Putting everything in Adopt at launch
Radar loses credibility as a discriminator; teams ignore it
Start honest: new tools begin in Assess unless previously evaluated
Never moving blips
The radar becomes stale and teams stop trusting it
Enforce 6-month review cadence with named accountable owners
Hold as a punishment
Vendors complain; teams treat Hold as political, not technical
Hold decisions must cite specific technical or security evidence
Radar maintained by one person
Single point of failure; reflects one person's opinions
Radar review is a multi-team Architecture Board process
No blip rationale
Teams can't make context-aware exceptions or understand ring criteria
Every blip entry must include a 'Why this ring' section with evidence
Integrating the Radar with Engineering Tooling

Publishing the radar as a static document is necessary but not sufficient. The most effective radars are wired into the tools engineers use daily, so the evaluated position surfaces at the moment of decision rather than requiring engineers to remember to look it up.
Radar-as-Code
Representing radar blips as structured data lets you generate the interactive diagram, validate RFC proposals, and produce changelogs from the same source of truth:
RFC Gate: Radar Status Check
Add an automated check to your RFC or pull-request workflow that warns engineers when they reference a Hold technology without an approved exception:
Radar Edition Changelog Template

Each radar edition should ship with a human-readable changelog. Engineering teams read the changelog to understand what changed and why — it is the primary mechanism for communicating architectural direction:

# Claude Ecosystem Tech Radar — Edition 2025-H2 Changelog

## Summary
- 1 new blip (MCP Servers added to ASSESS)
- 2 promotions (Claude Code: ASSESS → TRIAL; Prompt Caching: TRIAL → ADOPT)
- 1 demotion (Unvalidated third-party wrappers: ASSESS → HOLD)
- 12 blips reviewed with no change

## Promoted

### Claude Code: ASSESS → TRIAL
After an 8-week pilot with 4 teams (see pilot-report-2025-Q2), Claude Code
demonstrated measurable productivity gains in interactive developer workflows.
It is not yet recommended for unattended CI pipelines — a separate evaluation
is in progress (RFC-2142). Start with a 60-day team pilot with explicit success
criteria before broader rollout.

### Prompt Caching: TRIAL → ADOPT
Two platform teams ran prompt caching in production for 6 months with no
incidents. The API is stable, cost savings of 40–60% on large-context workloads
are confirmed, and our runbook covers the key failure mode (stale cache on
system prompt update). Standard for new large-context integrations.

## Demoted

### Unvalidated Third-Party Claude Wrappers: ASSESS → HOLD
Three wrappers evaluated in Q2 2025 had no published vulnerability policy,
depended on vendored copies of the Anthropic SDK that were 2+ versions behind,
and exposed credentials in debug logs. Use the official SDK with a thin internal
abstraction layer instead (see RFC-2041).

## New

### MCP Servers: ASSESS
Model Context Protocol is an emerging open standard for giving Claude access
to tools and data sources. The protocol is stabilizing but the ecosystem of
third-party MCP servers is immature with uneven security posture. Explore for
greenfield agentic projects; do not use community MCP servers without security
review. Watch for: protocol v1.0 GA release and internal MCP security checklist.
Practical Pitfalls When Starting a Radar
Treating ring assignment as permanent:
Blips must move as evidence accumulates. Schedule the first review 3 months after launch to build the habit.
Confusing 'popular' with 'Adopt':
A framework can be widely used in the industry and still be Assess in your context due to regulatory, security, or operational constraints specific to your organization.
Radar without a proposal process:
Engineers will shadow-adopt Assess technologies if there is no clear path to proposing a ring change. Define the PoC → RFC → architecture board path explicitly.
Forgetting to review after major Anthropic releases:
Claude model updates, new API features (extended thinking, new tool types), and SDK major versions should all trigger targeted blip reviews, not just the 6-month cadence.
Using the radar for vendor negotiation:

Hold placements based on commercial disagreements rather than technical evidence destroy the radar's credibility. All ring decisions must cite engineering or security evidence, not business relationships.
Summary

The Technology Radar provides a shared vocabulary — Adopt, Trial, Assess, Hold — for communicating evaluated positions on Claude ecosystem tools. Effective radars are updated on a regular cadence, governed by a multi-team architecture board, and embedded into the engineering workflow through ADRs, RFCs, and onboarding. Each blip entry requires evidence-based rationale so teams can make context-aware decisions and propose informed changes.
Adopt = production-proven with documented operational history; Trial = limited production with a time-boxed pilot
Assess = explore in PoC only; Hold = pause new adoption pending a specific trigger for re-evaluation
Move blips based on evidence: PoC results and production data drive promotion; security advisories drive demotion
Structure blip entries with: what it is, why this ring, how to use it (or watch for), and references
Publish the radar as an interactive artefact and link it from ADRs, RFCs, and engineer onboarding
Review the full radar every 6 months; place emergency Hold entries within 48 hours of a security incident
